title: practical english usage - Part 27
content:
The  meaning of a prepositional verb can be very different from the meanings of 2 idiomatic       meanings:      look     after,      get    over not Listen! (     Listen to!) Prepositions are not used when there is no object.

Luke walked down the road without looking at anybody.

You never listen to me. (     Y  never Usten-me.) Many English verbs are regularly followed by prepositions before objects.

1  verb    + preposition:        listen      to;  look     at 13 two-part      verbs:   prepositional         verbs For details of particular phrasal verbs, see a good dictionary.

or                                                              not Give me back my watch,  Give me my watch back, (     Gw^back tw my switched?) not Is that the light which you switched off? (     .. .thelighl off which-you not She switched it off (     She switehed-offiL) But they can only go after pronoun objects.

She switched off the light,  She switched the light off adverbs). ►     196.1 Adverb particles can go either before or after noun objects (unlike most 4 word    order     with    objects Could you switch the light off?  I helped Anna to clean up the room.

Others are transitive.

I got up at 7.00 today. That colour really stands out.

Some phrasal verbs are intransitive (they do not have objects).

3 phrasal      verbs     with    and   without      objects 1 Verbs    Section grammar •  14   verbs of movement: she ran in, etc For details, ►    Section 2.

What have you done? I realised that I hadn't turned the lights off.

Have is used to make perfect forms.

Do you smoke? It didn't matter. Do come in.

auxiliary verbs.

Do is used to make questions, negatives and emphatic forms of non­ Is it raining? She was imprisoned by a military court.

Be is added to other verbs to make progressive and passive forms.

2 be,   do   and   have verbs are added to other verbs. There are two groups.

sees, seeing, saw, seen). So to express all these meanings, 'auxiliary' (or 'helping') many different one-word forms: the maximum (except for be) is five (e.g. see, repetition, willingness, possibility, obligation. But English verbs do not have phrase - for example questioning, negation, time, completion, continuation, In English sentences, a lot of important meanings are expressed by the verb 1  the   need    for   auxiliary       verbs 15  verb    + verb:   auxiliary      verbs They crawled out of the cellar.  The eagle flew away.

(More natural than I came down the stairs jumping.) I jumped down the stairs.

(More natural than She crossed the garden dancing.) She danced across the garden.

I walked back. (More natural than I went back walking.) In English, the third of these solutions is the most common.

She ran in.

describe the direction separately: Or  we  can use a verb which  makes clear the nature of the movement, and She entered running.

of the movement separately: We   can use a verb which  includes the idea of direction, and describe the nature She came in running.

We   can use three separate words for the three ideas: several possibilities.

When  we  want to talk about a movement, its direction and its nature, there are 14 verbs     of  movement:       she    ran    in,   etc For details of particular prepositional verbs, see a good dictionary.

For prepositional verbs in the passive, ►    62.

For the difference between prepositions and adverb particles, ►     195.

I'm looking forward to the party.

She went up to the officer and explained her problem.

I'll think about it and get back to you.

If you're on the road on Saturday night, look out for drunk drivers.

It's hard to put up with people who won't stop talking.

grammar ♦   16   verb + verb: other structures I don't want to have to get her to start telling lies.             •> He seems to be trying to sit up.

I keep forgetting to go shopping. Don't let me stop you working.

It is possible to have 'chains' of verbs following each other.

My keys seem to have disappeared.

We're starting to get invited to some of the neighbours' parties.

I happened to see Alice the other day.
